title: Big-O in Practice — What Java Developers Actually Need

sections.heading: Why it matters in interviews
sections.content:
Senior engineers aren't asked to prove O(n log n) for mergesort. They're asked:
• "Why is this endpoint slow?"
• "Will this scale when we have 10x users?"
• "What's the cost of this loop inside a loop?"

The answer always comes back to understanding which operations are O(1), O(log n), O(n), or O(n²) in the code you write daily.

sections.heading: Java Collections — operation costs
sections.content:
// HashMap — O(1) average for get/put/containsKey
Map<String, Delivery> cache = new HashMap<>();
cache.get("D-42");           // O(1) — hash + equals
cache.put("D-43", delivery); // O(1) amortized

// TreeMap — O(log n) for get/put (sorted)
TreeMap<Instant, Event> timeline = new TreeMap<>();
timeline.subMap(from, to);   // O(log n + k) — k = results

// ArrayList — O(1) random access, O(n) search
List<Driver> drivers = new ArrayList<>();
drivers.get(5);              // O(1) — array index
drivers.contains(driver);    // O(n) — linear scan!
drivers.add(driver);         // O(1) amortized, O(n) worst (resize)

// LinkedList — O(1) add/remove at ends, O(n) random access
Deque<Task> queue = new LinkedList<>();
queue.addFirst(task);        // O(1)
queue.get(50);               // O(n) — must traverse!
Senior insight: Knowing these costs helps you spot performance bugs instantly. If you see .contains() inside a loop on an ArrayList — that's O(n²). Switch to a HashSet.

sections.heading: Common complexity traps in Spring Boot
sections.content:
// ❌ O(n²) — contains() inside loop
public List<Delivery> filterActive(List<Delivery> all, List<String> activeIds) {
    return all.stream()
        .filter(d -> activeIds.contains(d.getId())) // O(n) per element!
        .toList();
}
// With 10K deliveries × 5K activeIds = 50M comparisons

// ✅ O(n) — HashSet lookup
public List<Delivery> filterActive(List<Delivery> all, List<String> activeIds) {
    Set<String> idSet = new HashSet<>(activeIds); // O(n) once
    return all.stream()
        .filter(d -> idSet.contains(d.getId()))    // O(1) per element
        .toList();
}
// ❌ O(n) per request — loading all entities then filtering
@GetMapping("/deliveries")
public List<DeliveryDTO> getByStatus(@RequestParam String status) {
    return repo.findAll().stream()   // loads ALL from DB
        .filter(d -> d.getStatus().equals(status))
        .map(this::toDto).toList();
}

// ✅ O(log n) — let the database do the filtering
@GetMapping("/deliveries")
public List<DeliveryDTO> getByStatus(@RequestParam String status) {
    return repo.findByStatus(status).stream() // indexed query
        .map(this::toDto).toList();
}

sections.heading: Amortized complexity — why ArrayList.add is O(1)
sections.content: ArrayList doubles its internal array when full. One expensive O(n) copy happens, but the next n adds are O(1). Averaged out = O(1) amortized. Same principle applies to: • HashMap rehashing (at load factor 0.75) • StringBuilder internal buffer expansion • Kafka producer batch accumulation Interview tip: When asked "Is ArrayList.add O(1) or O(n)?" — the correct answer is "O(1) amortized, O(n) worst case during resize."

title: Collections Deep Dive — Choosing the Right Structure

sections.heading: Decision matrix
sections.content: When to use what: | Need | Structure | Why | |------|-----------|-----| | Fast lookup by key | HashMap | O(1) get/put | | Sorted iteration | TreeMap | O(log n), natural order | | Unique elements | HashSet | O(1) contains | | Insertion order preserved | LinkedHashMap | O(1) + order | | FIFO processing | ArrayDeque | O(1) add/poll, no sync overhead | | Thread-safe map | ConcurrentHashMap | Lock striping, no global lock | | Thread-safe queue | BlockingQueue | Producer-consumer pattern | | Immutable snapshot | List.of() / Map.of() | No defensive copy needed |

sections.heading: HashMap internals (interview favorite)
sections.content:
// How HashMap works:
// 1. hashCode() → bucket index
// 2. If collision → linked list in bucket
// 3. Java 8+: if bucket > 8 entries → tree (O(log n) instead of O(n))

// Why hashCode + equals contract matters:
public class DriverKey {
    private final String id;
    private final String region;

    @Override
    public int hashCode() {
        return Objects.hash(id, region); // MUST use same fields as equals
    }

    @Override
    public boolean equals(Object o) {
        if (this == o) return true;
        if (!(o instanceof DriverKey dk)) return false;
        return id.equals(dk.id) && region.equals(dk.region);
    }
}

// ❌ BROKEN: mutable field used in hashCode
public class MutableKey {
    private String name; // can change after put!
    public int hashCode() { return name.hashCode(); }
    // If name changes → object is in wrong bucket → lost forever
}
Load factor 0.75: When 75% of buckets are filled, HashMap doubles and rehashes everything. That's why initial capacity matters for known-size maps: 
// Avoid multiple rehashes for 1000 entries
Map<String, Driver> map = new HashMap<>(1400); // 1000/0.75 ≈ 1334

sections.heading: ConcurrentHashMap — Spring Boot multi-threaded context
sections.content:
// Spring Boot runs requests in parallel → shared state needs safety

// ❌ Race condition
private Map<String, AtomicInteger> counters = new HashMap<>();
public void increment(String key) {
    counters.computeIfAbsent(key, k -> new AtomicInteger()).incrementAndGet();
    // HashMap.computeIfAbsent is NOT thread-safe!
}

// ✅ Thread-safe
private ConcurrentHashMap<String, AtomicInteger> counters = new ConcurrentHashMap<>();
public void increment(String key) {
    counters.computeIfAbsent(key, k -> new AtomicInteger()).incrementAndGet();
    // ConcurrentHashMap.computeIfAbsent IS atomic for the key
}
Key insight: ConcurrentHashMap uses lock striping (segments), not a single global lock. Multiple threads can write to different segments simultaneously.

title: Recursion & Memoization — From Theory to Production

sections.heading: The pattern
Recursion in production code
Memoization → Caching (same concept, production scale)

title: Trees in Real Systems — B-Tree, Trie, and Why It Matters

sections.heading: B-Tree — every database index
sections.content:
When you create a PostgreSQL/MySQL index, it's a B-Tree: 
CREATE INDEX idx_delivery_status ON delivery(status, scheduled_at);

-- What happens internally:
-- B-Tree with branching factor ~100-500
-- Each node = one disk page (8KB)
-- Depth = log₁₀₀(rows) ≈ 3-4 levels for millions of rows
-- → Finding a row = 3-4 disk reads!

-- That's why indexed lookup is O(log n)
-- And full table scan is O(n) — must read ALL pages
Senior connection: • Composite index order matters (leftmost prefix rule) because B-Tree is sorted left-to-right • Range queries (BETWEEN, >, <) only benefit the LAST column in composite index • That's why you put equality columns first, range/sort columns last

sections.heading: TreeMap — sorted data in Java
sections.content:
// TreeMap = Red-Black Tree (self-balancing BST)
// Use when you need sorted access or range queries IN MEMORY

// Use case: time-windowed event store
TreeMap<Instant, DeliveryEvent> events = new TreeMap<>();

// Get all events in last hour — O(log n + k)
Instant oneHourAgo = Instant.now().minus(Duration.ofHours(1));
SortedMap<Instant, DeliveryEvent> recent = events.tailMap(oneHourAgo);

// Get first/last — O(log n)
DeliveryEvent earliest = events.firstEntry().getValue();
DeliveryEvent latest = events.lastEntry().getValue();

// vs HashMap: these operations would require sorting all entries
When TreeMap over HashMap: • Need sorted iteration • Need range queries (subMap, headMap, tailMap) • Need floor/ceiling (nearest key) When HashMap over TreeMap: • Only need get/put by exact key (O(1) vs O(log n)) • No ordering requirement

sections.heading: Trie — autocomplete and routing
sections.content:
// Trie (prefix tree) — O(m) lookup where m = key length
// Used in: autocomplete, IP routing, URL matching

// Spring Boot URL routing uses a similar structure internally:
// /api/deliveries/{id}
// /api/deliveries/{id}/status
// /api/drivers/{id}
// → tree-like dispatch: /api → deliveries|drivers → {id} → status

// Autocomplete example:
public class AutocompleteService {
    private final TrieNode root = new TrieNode();

    public void index(String word) {
        TrieNode node = root;
        for (char c : word.toCharArray()) {
            node = node.children.computeIfAbsent(c, k -> new TrieNode());
        }
        node.isWord = true;
    }

    public List<String> suggest(String prefix, int limit) {
        TrieNode node = root;
        for (char c : prefix.toCharArray()) {
            node = node.children.get(c);
            if (node == null) return List.of(); // no match
        }
        return collectWords(node, prefix, limit);
    }
}

sections.heading: Queue — from data structure to Kafka
Stack — call stack, undo, and parsing
sections.content:
// The JVM call stack IS a stack data structure:
// main() → deliveryService.create() → validator.validate() → ...
// StackOverflowError = recursion too deep (stack exhausted)

// Practical use: undo operations
Deque<Command> undoStack = new ArrayDeque<>();

public void execute(Command cmd) {
    cmd.execute();
    undoStack.push(cmd); // LIFO
}

public void undo() {
    if (!undoStack.isEmpty()) {
        Command last = undoStack.pop();
        last.undo();
    }
}

// Practical use: DFS traversal (explicit stack instead of recursion)
public List<Category> dfs(Category root) {
    Deque<Category> stack = new ArrayDeque<>();
    List<Category> visited = new ArrayList<>();
    stack.push(root);
    while (!stack.isEmpty()) {
        Category current = stack.pop();
        visited.add(current);
        current.getChildren().forEach(stack::push);
    }
    return visited;
}

sections.heading: Priority Queue — task scheduling
sections.content:
// PriorityQueue = heap data structure = O(log n) insert/poll
// Use case: process deliveries by priority

PriorityQueue<Delivery> priorityQueue = new PriorityQueue<>(
    Comparator.comparing(Delivery::getPriority)
             .thenComparing(Delivery::getScheduledAt)
);

priorityQueue.offer(expressDelivery);   // O(log n)
priorityQueue.offer(standardDelivery);  // O(log n)

Delivery next = priorityQueue.poll();   // always highest priority, O(log n)
// → expressDelivery comes first regardless of insertion order

// Same concept in distributed systems:
// Kafka: partition by priority level
// Spring @Async: PriorityBlockingQueue as thread pool queue

title: Hashing & Equality — From hashCode to Kafka Partitioning

sections.heading: hashCode/equals contract
sections.content:
// THE RULES:
// 1. If a.equals(b) → a.hashCode() == b.hashCode()  (MUST)
// 2. If a.hashCode() == b.hashCode() → a.equals(b) is NOT guaranteed (collisions exist)
// 3. hashCode must be consistent within same JVM run
// 4. Only use IMMUTABLE fields in hashCode

// ✅ Correct: Java record (auto-generates correct hashCode/equals)
public record DeliveryId(String region, Long sequence) {}
// Records generate: equals on all fields, hashCode on all fields

// ✅ Correct: manual implementation
@Entity
public class Driver {
    @Id private Long id;
    private String licenseNumber;

    @Override
    public boolean equals(Object o) {
        if (this == o) return true;
        if (!(o instanceof Driver d)) return false;
        return Objects.equals(id, d.id); // use business key or PK
    }

    @Override
    public int hashCode() {
        return Objects.hash(id); // same field as equals!
    }
}

// ❌ BROKEN: uses mutable field
public int hashCode() {
    return Objects.hash(status); // status changes → object lost in HashMap!
}

sections.heading: Consistent hashing — Kafka partition assignment
Equals in JPA — the entity identity trap
sections.content:
// JPA entities: equals/hashCode is TRICKY
// Problem: ID is null before persist!

// ❌ Using @Id before persist
@Entity
public class Delivery {
    @Id @GeneratedValue private Long id;

    public boolean equals(Object o) {
        if (!(o instanceof Delivery d)) return false;
        return Objects.equals(id, d.id); // both null before persist → all equal!
    }
}

// ✅ Option 1: Natural/business key
public boolean equals(Object o) {
    if (!(o instanceof Delivery d)) return false;
    return Objects.equals(trackingNumber, d.trackingNumber);
}

// ✅ Option 2: UUID assigned at construction
@Entity
public class Delivery {
    @Id @GeneratedValue private Long id;
    @Column(unique = true)
    private final UUID uuid = UUID.randomUUID(); // set at creation!

    public boolean equals(Object o) {
        if (!(o instanceof Delivery d)) return false;
        return uuid.equals(d.uuid);
    }
    public int hashCode() { return uuid.hashCode(); }
}
Interview tip: This is a frequently asked question for Java senior roles. Show you understand WHY the default Object.equals breaks with JPA detachment/merge cycles.
